data.title: Vivos targets cash flow positive operations by end of 2026 as it scales SO teams with $500,000+ monthly collections potential
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Vivos Therapeutics (VVOS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • “2025 was a pivotal year for Vivos,” said (Co-Founder, Chairman of the Board & CEO R. Kirk Huntsman), adding that the company “proved our core thesis around patient demand and preference for our Vivos method” and said the SCN rollout “has proven to be everything we expected it to be.”
  • (CEO Huntsman) tied the operating model shift directly to profitability goals, saying “recently announced significant cost savings initiatives and strengthened capital structure have now positioned Vivos to drive higher top line growth, better contribution margins and a clear path forward towards our goal of cash flow positive operations by the end of this year.”
  • (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Bradford Amman) said the “full year 2025 revenue increase of $2.4 million or 16%” was driven by “an increase of approximately $4.8 million in sleep testing services and an increase of approximately $2.2 million of revenue generated from the treatment to patients launched at 2 of SCN’s 7 sleep center locations,” while noting the pivot “fully expected revenue from these legacy programs to decline.”
  • (CEO Huntsman) emphasized capacity and payer access as near-term gating items, saying the SCN limiting constraints were “insufficient physical space,” “an adequate number of providers and staff,” and “customary issues with third-party payer credentialing,” while adding SCN is now receiving “notices of in-network status with a number of commercial health insurance payers, along with participating status with Medicare.”

Outlook

  • (CEO Huntsman) framed near-term ramp timing around staffing and payer access: “the full impact of having replaced these doctors… will start to be seen towards the end of Q1 and then into Q2,” and added that payer participation “just started to fold into the revenue productivity stream in the latter part of Q1… but most of the impact is going to be in Q2 forward.”
  • (CEO Huntsman) outlined unit economics targets tied to staffing deployment: “each SO team could potentially generate collections well in excess of $500,000 per month… with contribution margins well above 50%,” and separately said Nevada revenue per case “averages just under $5,000 with contribution margins above 50%.”
  • (CEO Huntsman) described the expansion path as more capital-efficient affiliations, stating “typical capital outlays for an affiliation are under $1 million… affiliations typically preserve about 75% to 80% of the economics for the company.”

Financial Results

  • (CFO Amman) reported 2025 gross profit of “$10.5 million” vs. “$9 million” in 2024, with gross margin “constant at 60%” in both years, while cost of sales rose “approximately $900,000 or 15% to $6.9 million” on higher diagnostic services and staffing for sleep center affiliations.
  • (CFO Amman) said operating expenses rose to “$30.4 million” from “$20.2 million,” driven by G&A of “$27.7 million” including “$6.7 million in costs associated with running SCN operations,” plus “approximately $1.6 million related to professional fees, most of which were onetime expenses.”
  • (CFO Amman) said net loss increased to “$21.2 million,” and cash usage included “net cash used in operating activities… approximately $15.3 million” vs. “$12.7 million” in 2024; year-end cash was “approximately $2 million.”
  • (CFO Amman) highlighted balance sheet pressure and funding actions: total liabilities were “approximately $26.7 million” vs. “$7.3 million” a year earlier, and the company raised “$4.6 million” in gross proceeds from a January 16, 2026 warrant inducement and “$2.25 million” on April 7, 2026 from New Seneca Partners, while noting the need to “continue to augment with additional equity financing… to stay in compliance with NASDAQ’s minimum stockholders’ equity requirement.”

Q&A

  • Scott Henry, Alliance Global Partners: “you did about $6.8 million in Q3… dropped to $3.8 million in Q4… what happened in Q4… [and] your thoughts on Q1?” (CEO Huntsman) answered, “our new model is highly dependent upon total doctor days,” and said Q4 was impacted because “we lost a few providers that we didn’t expect to lose,” adding that in-network payer access began contributing late in Q1 and “most of the impact is going to be in Q2 forward.”
  • Henry, Alliance Global Partners: what revenue level is needed for cash flow positive and what about “$8.3 million in current portion of long-term debt”? (CFO Amman) said that beyond cost actions, “we need to be close to double [the 2025 $17 million] by 2027… on a net income positive basis,” and on debt said “the maturity date on that is in 2026,” adding the company could “roll it over or pay it off.”
  • Robert Sassoon, Water Tower Research: growth in diagnostic/treatment under the new model and lessons from SCN integration? (CFO Amman) said sleep testing service revenue was “about $6 million” in 2025 and treatment revenue was “$2.2 million,” with legacy declines expected to “start to roll off” in 2026; (CEO Huntsman) said lessons included working “in a collaborative manner” with physicians, navigating payers, and said Vivos is “contracting with a national firm” to accelerate in-network participation.
  • Sassoon, Water Tower Research: traction from SoundHealth and long-term growth prospects? (CEO Huntsman) said “it’s still fairly early,” but “we’re having trouble keeping those… units in stock,” and on growth said “we sort of bet the farm here on… SCN… that bet is paying off,” while also citing inbound interest from specialty groups and saying affiliations are “a fraction of what it costs for us to acquire.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on execution volatility and financing, with questions pointing to Q4 revenue drop, cash flow thresholds, and near-term debt maturity, including “what happened in Q4” and whether the debt would be “rolling… over.”
  • Management tone was slightly positive and forward-leaning, using confidence language such as “we feel very, very good,” “we remedied it,” and “we are more optimistic… than we probably have ever been,” while acknowledging an operational surprise: “we got caught a little by surprise in Q4.”
  • Compared with Q3’s “watershed quarter” framing and emphasis on rapid ramp, Q4 commentary added more discussion of provider attrition, payer credentialing, and explicit balance-sheet constraints tied to NASDAQ equity compliance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 emphasized first full quarter post-SCN with revenue “increased 76% to $6.8 million,” while Q4 discussion centered on a sequential decline referenced in Q&A (“dropped to $3.8 million in Q4”) and management attributing it to lost “doctor days” from unexpected provider absences.
  • Q3 highlighted demand outstripping capacity and a credentialing ramp, while Q4 updated that payer participation improved (“in-network status… along with… Medicare”) and that provider redundancy was added to avoid repeat disruptions.
  • Q3 commentary focused on scaling SAMC/SO teams and ramp economics; Q4 reinforced those economics with a quantified claim that each SO team could generate “well in excess of $500,000 per month,” while adding capital structure actions and NASDAQ equity compliance needs.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CEO Huntsman) identified operational bottlenecks as “physical space,” “providers and staff recruiting, training and onboarding,” and “third-party payer credentialing,” and warned revenue sensitivity to staffing: “When a dentist doesn’t show up for a day, it can be… $15,000, $20,000, $30,000 or more of lost production.”
  • (CFO Amman) pointed to liquidity and listing-related constraints, saying additional equity financing is needed “to stay in compliance with NASDAQ’s minimum stockholders’ equity requirement,” alongside debt maturities reclassified as current because “the maturity date on that is in 2026.”

Final Takeaway

Management portrayed 2025 as the first year the SCN-centered MSO/DSO model began to translate into higher diagnostic and treatment revenue, while also acknowledging that the model’s output is tightly linked to provider availability and payer credentialing timelines. Executives emphasized improved in-network coverage and staffing redundancy as key catalysts for a stronger Q1-to-Q2 revenue trajectory, and reiterated a target of cash flow positive operations by the end of 2026, while highlighting near-term financing needs tied to NASDAQ stockholders’ equity compliance and 2026 debt maturities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Masimo lands $100M contract modification for monitoring deal
data.description:
• Masimo (MASI) was awarded a maximum ~$100M fixed-price modification contract for patient monitoring and capital equipment.
  • The award exercises the five-year option period under its existing five-year base + one five-year extension contract.
  • The ordering period runs through April 19, 2031, and supports customers across the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and federal civilian agencies.
  • The deal is funded through FY2026–2031 defense working capital funds, with the Defense Logistics Agency Troop Support being the contracting authority.

data.title: ESCO Technologies to buy Megger Group in $2.35B cash and stock deal
data.description:
ESCO Technologies (ESE) down 2.7% post-market Wednesday after saying it agreed to acquire Megger Group Ltd. from TBG AG for $2.35B, consisting of $900M in cash and ESCO equity valued at ~$1.4B.

Megger is a global provider of testing, monitoring, and data-driven solutions for utilities and critical electric infrastructure, including industrial, transportation, data center, and renewable end markets; it will become part of ESCO's (ESE) Utility Solution Group segment.

Megger's products and services include battery, cable, circuit breaker, relay, transformer, and motor test equipment, on-line monitoring solutions, and data analytics for grid and electric power assets; FY 2026 revenues are expected to total ~$590M.

ESCO (ESE) also issued upside guidance for Q2, seeing adjusted earnings of $1.91/share and revenues of $309M, above FactSet analyst consensus of $1.77/share and $307.6M, respectively.

data.title: Gevo withdraws from DoE loan program, will seek alternative funding for ATJ-30 project
data.description:
Gevo (GEVO) down 11.6% post-market Wednesday after saying it withdrew its loan guarantee application with the U.S. Department of Energy Office of Energy Dominance Financing and will pursue other funding opportunities for its ATJ-30 sustainable aviation fuel project by the end of 2026.

Gevo (GEVO) said it pulled its application after discussions with the DoE office revealed that required business objectives, specifically supporting enhanced oil recovery operations, are not yet commercially viable at scale in the project area and that there are opportunities for alternative financing better aligned with company strategy.

The company had previously received a conditional loan commitment from the DoE for its ATJ-60 project in South Dakota and was seeking to transition the commitment to the smaller ATJ-30 project at its North Dakota facility in Richardton.

"Over the past year, we have substantially derisked our position in low-carbon ethanol required for our ATJ, and we believe we are in a better position to secure alternative financing that allows us to pursue the most accretive business case in alignment with company strategy and timelines," Gevo (GEVO) CEO Paul Bloom said.

data.title: Raytheon wins $234.8M contract for AMRAAM ER production shift
data.description:
• Raytheon (RTX) was awarded a not-to-exceed $234.8M undefinitized contract for the AMRAAM Extended Range (ER) transition to production program.
  • The contract supports activities required to transition AMRAAM ER into full-rate production.
  • The work will be performed in Tucson, Arizona, and is expected to be completed by April 13, 2030.
  • FMS funds of $61.6M were obligated at award, and the Air Force Lifecycle Management Center Weapons Superiority Contracting Branch is the contracting activity.

data.title: Silver heads for sixth straight annual deficit with drawdown raising squeeze risk
data.description:
The silver market faces a ‌sixth consecutive year of structural deficit, with 762M oz drawn from inventories since 2021, raising the risk of a renewed liquidity squeeze despite weaker demand expectations, the Silver Institute and consultancy Metals Focus said Wednesday.

The global silver market deficit is expected to widen to 46.3M oz ​in 2026 from 40.3M oz ​in 2025, even as ⁠total demand falls 2% due to weaker industrial and jewelry consumption, partly offset by stronger coin and bar demand, the research showed.

Liquidity has improved since last October's squeeze, as metal flowed back from the U.S., silver-backed ​exchange-traded products saw outflows, and Indian demand eased, "but risks of another liquidity squeeze this year remain," said Philip Newman, managing director at Metals Focus, which prepared the research for the ​Silver Institute.

Conditions for a silver squeeze will be created again, requiring further outflows from the U.S., if the price becomes more volatile and Indian demand gets active, especially coupled with inflows to ETPs storing their metal in London, Newman said.

Gold futures hovered ~$4,800/oz and silver at nearly $80/oz on Wednesday, as hopes for a fresh round of talks between the U.S. and Iran eased inflation concerns tied to higher energy prices.

"A softer dollar has supported the recovery; however, a sustained breakout likely depends on further constructive developments in the Middle East," Saxo Bank analysts said in a note.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery finished down 0.5% to $4,800.00/oz, while front-month Comex April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ticked up 0.1% to $79.491/oz.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (DUST), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Goldstorm Metals upsizes private placement to $7 million
data.description:
• Goldstorm Metals (GSTM:CA) (FSE: B2U) on Wednesday said it has increased the size of its non-brokered private placement to up to $7 million from $4 million, citing strong investor demand.
  • The offering includes multiple unit types priced between $0.20 and $0.31, each consisting of common shares and warrants with an exercise price of $0.30 and a 36-month term.
  • Proceeds from certain units will be used for exploration spending qualifying under Canadian tax rules, while remaining funds will be used for general corporate purposes.
  • The offering is expected to close around the week of April 30, subject to regulatory approvals.
  • NEM +0.23% after hours to $113.3.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Lockheed Martin Space secures $850M U.S. Navy contract modification
data.description:
• Lockheed Martin Space, a business area of Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT), won an $850.41M contract modification for the TRIDENT II (D5) Life Extension 2 program, covering advanced design and development work.
  • The work is expected to be completed by September 30, 2030.
  • FY2026 U.S. Navy weapons procurement funds of  $850.41M will be obligated at the time of award, with no funds expiring in the current fiscal year.
  • The Strategic Systems Programs is the contracting activity.

data.title: Stocks to watch after market on Wednesday: CDNA, JBHT, SLG, QDEL
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages advanced on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) ending at an all-time high as investors priced in good news on the U.S.-Iran conflict front while parsing more bank and financial earnings.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) closed at the record of 7,022.93 points, ending +0.8%.

Here are the four stocks to watch on Wednesday after market:

CareDx (CDNA) surged over 8% in extended trading Wednesday after announcing it will divest its Lab Products business to EuroBio Scientific for $170 million in cash. The precision medicine company also disclosed preliminary first-quarter revenue of approximately $118 million, well ahead of the $102.49 million Wall Street consensus and representing 39% year-over-year growth.

J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT) gained 1.3% in postmarket trading after the logistics company topped analyst expectations with its fiscal first-quarter results. The company reported GAAP EPS of $1.49, beating the $1.46 consensus estimate, while operating revenue rose 5% year-over-year to $3.06 billion.

SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) sank 4.0% in after-hours trading after Manhattan’s largest office landlord posted first-quarter FFO per share of $0.84, missing the $1.08 consensus estimate. Revenue of $253.1 million did top the $242.4 million expectation, and the company signed 51 Manhattan office leases covering 929,264 square feet during the quarter, its highest first-quarter volume on record.

QuidelOrtho (QDEL) tumbled approximately 11% following the market close after issuing preliminary first-quarter revenue guidance with a midpoint of $617.5 million, well below the $679.59 million consensus. The diagnostics company attributed the shortfall to a weaker respiratory season, with U.S. influenza-like illness visits down roughly 30% year-over-year, along with proposed reimbursement changes in China and delayed orders in EMEA due to the Middle East conflict.
